text: Hi everyone, welcome to This is my Architecture. I'm Jasmine and I have here with me Nikhil Sharma
from OLX Autos. Hi Nikhil, thanks for joining us. Hi Jasmine, thank you, thank you for having me.
Tell us about OLX Autos. At OLX Autos we offer a one-stop solution to buy and sell used vehicles
from having an online evaluation of your car to have an own inspection. We offer anything and
everything. Okay, and do you have global presence?
Yes, we do. As of now we are operational in 11 countries including India, Indonesia, South
America and Turkey. And what are the challenges of going global?
So there have been countless challenges. A few of the challenges we have solved is by building
an internal developer platform tool because primarily one of the challenges was to reduce
our time to market and accelerate our deployment velocity. Okay, and so if I as a user have
to create the platform, what's there under the hood of this architecture?
Okay, so as an end user if you want to have an environment, you just go to the Orion portal
and you'll just go with the... And then once you have the... We also open up the Orion portal,
and then the DNS resolution goes to the Amazon Rout of 53. We are using it for the DNS resolution.
And then it resolves the DNS to the Amazon Application Load Balancer. And then the Amazon
Load Application Load Balancer is going to send the request to our Kubernetes pods. We are using Amazon EKS
as a container orchestrator. And then all of our workload is running on AWS EKS.
So when you are creating a request to our new environment, you will mention some details like
name of the environment, the business use case of the environment, and the business vertical that you
want to create for which this environment is needed. And then this request goes to our application pod
that is running on Amazon EKS and underlying it is using spot instances.
Okay. And what percent of spot do you use?
As of now, we are using 100% spot instances.
Wow. That's amazing. So how about the cost optimization that you've achieved with 100%?
So the use case that we had is that we are creating the replicas of Olex orders environments.
And we had to keep the cost in check because if we're creating too many replicas, we obviously
increase in the cost exponentially. So that's why we just finalized that we are going to go with
100% spot for the efficient cost utilization. And we just have to figure out how we can leverage
spot to its maximum capacity. Okay. And as you are leveraging spot, you also need to take care
of spot termination, right? How do you do that? Yes. Yes. So for that, we are using AWS node
termination handler. So whenever Amazon is about to terminate any spot instances, it senses an event
which AWS termination handler is listening to and then it drains out that nodes. So all the workload
that was running on that spot instances gets shifted to another node. So would not experience any disruption
in the business. Got it. So basically the request comes to the ports which are deployed on spot,
right? And this is orchestrated by Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service. Exactly.
Okay. And how does Amazon EKS help you? It helps us by offloading the operational overhead
and it is helping us by handling the MS load that we are putting on it. We started with the 100 ports
and now we are running with 10,000 plus ports. So we are incrementally stress testing Amazon EKS.
Wow. And that's quite a journey, right? Starting from 100 ports to 10,000 ports.
Yes. So that's amazing. And how about this Amazon SNS and SKS? What is the role of these two?
Okay. So when a user is trying to making a request to create an environment, ultimately what it's
requesting is I need a bunch of microservices for that business vertical. For example, if you are creating
an environment for business vertical X, it may need 70 plus microservices. So this our main application
running as a pod, it is going to create the number of jobs, Kubernetes jobs for each microservice. For
example, if you're launching three microservices, then it will create a job that will take care of
launching microservice one, launching microservice two, launching microservice three.
Now, as we are running everything on spot, there is a strong likelihood that before the time this job
gets completed, spot instances terminates the underlying node and this job gets disrupted. EKS
takes care of replacing this job by another job, but ultimately the process was not able to finish up
the task that it was assigned to. So what we are doing is whenever a job gets triggered
to create the microservice, it sends an event to Amazon SNS and then Amazon SNS finds out that message
to SQS. So the new job that is getting replaced by this one watches out the AWS SQS and notices,
okay, there is an unfinished job and that job was supposed to launch microservice one, but it failed
to do so due to XYZ reason. So that takes care of launching and finishing the job.
Okay, great. So you have different jobs spinning up different microservices.
Yes.
Right? Every job stores the state through SNS to SQS.
Yes.
And when the job comes up, the new job comes up, it also checks SQS if there is anything
pending to be processed.
Exactly. So it watches out SQS so that we are having consistent state of the provisioning and
maintenance of environments.
Okay. And what's the benefit of this architecture?
So with this architecture, we are able to increase our deployment velocity and our global expansion.
In December 2021, we are operating in nine markets. Now we are operating in 11 markets,
such as well. And it also helped in reducing our time to market, shipping up our features faster to
production.
Amazing. Thank you so much, Nikhil, for sharing this architecture.
It's a pleasure, Jasmine.
Thank you.
Thank you. And thank you everyone for joining us for This is My Architecture.
Thank you.
